perturbation.original_text: This Confidentiality Agreement and Agreement for the Release of Due Diligence Materials and Waiver of Claims (“Agreement”) is dated ____________________, by (“Buyer”) regarding the Dollar General Portfolio (the “Property”).
perturbation.changed_text: This Confidentiality Agreement and Agreement for the Release of Due Diligence Materials and Waiver of Claims (“Agreement”) is dated ____________________, by (“Buyer”) regarding the Dollar General Portfolio (the “Land”).
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Property' to 'Land' creates potential ambiguity and conflict. While often used interchangeably in common language, legal definitions can differ significantly, especially concerning the inclusion of improvements or fixtures. 'Land' in a legal context often refers solely to the unimproved earth.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 659 defines real property as land, that which is affixed to it, that which is incidental or appurtenant to it, and that which is immovable by law. By using 'Land' instead of 'Property,' the agreement becomes ambiguous as to whether it includes buildings and other improvements on the land, potentially leading to disputes over the scope of the agreement. This creates a legal contradiction because the agreement initially intends to cover the entire 'Property' including all improvements, but the modified term 'Land' narrows this scope under CA law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 659 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 2. PROPERTY [654 - 1422] ( Heading of Division 2 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 13. ) PART 1. PROPERTY IN GENERAL [654 - 749] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1. NATURE OF PROPERTY [654 - 663] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 659. Land is the material of the earth, whatever may be the ingredients of which it is composed, whether soil, rock, or other substance, and includes free or occupied space for an indefinite distance upwards as well as downwards, subject to limitations upon the use of airspace imposed, and rights in the use of airspace granted, by law. (Amended by Stats. 1963, Ch. 860.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Property' to 'Land' introduces a legal contradiction because 'Land' has a narrower legal definition than 'Property,' especially concerning improvements. California Civil Code § 659 defines land as 'the material of the earth,' including soil and rock, but the Law Explanation highlights that 'Property' generally includes buildings and other improvements. By using 'Land,' the agreement becomes ambiguous, potentially excluding improvements and leading to disputes over the scope of the agreement, contradicting the original intent to cover the entire 'Property,' which includes improvements, making the modified term 'Land' narrower under CA law.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer has been advised that The Kase Group (“TKG”) is acting on behalf of Owner as broker (“Broker”) in connection with the proposed sale of the Property, and Owner has agreed to pay a commission to TKG upon a sale of the Property in accordance with a separate agreement between Owner and TKG.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer has been advised that The Kase Group (“TKG”) is acting on behalf of Owner as finder (“Broker”) in connection with the proposed sale of the Property, and Owner has agreed to pay a commission to TKG upon a sale of the Property in accordance with a separate agreement between Owner and TKG.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing the term 'broker' with 'finder' could create ambiguity regarding the duties and responsibilities of The Kase Group (TKG). While both facilitate transactions, their legal obligations differ. A 'broker' typically has fiduciary duties, while a 'finder' might not.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 10131 defines the activities that require a real estate license, including acting as a real estate broker. A 'finder' may or may not be subject to these licensing requirements depending on the scope of their activities. By using 'finder' instead of 'broker,' the agreement introduces uncertainty about whether TKG is operating legally and fulfilling the duties expected of a licensed real estate broker under California law. The change can lead to the contract being challenged based on misrepresentation of TKG's role and qualifications.

REAL ESTATE [10000 - 11506] ( Division 4 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) PART 1. LICENSING OF PERSONS [10000 - 10580] ( Part 1 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) CHAPTER 3. Real Estate Regulations [10130 - 10249.93] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) ARTICLE 1. Scope of Regulation [10130 - 10149] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) 10131. A real estate broker within the meaning of this part is a person who, for a compensation or in expectation of a compensation, regardless of the form or time of payment, does or negotiates to do one or more of the following acts for another or others: (a) Sells or offers to sell, buys or offers to buy, solicits prospective sellers or buyers of, solicits or obtains listings of, or negotiates the purchase, sale, or exchange of real property or a business opportunity. (b) Leases or rents or offers to lease or rent, or places for rent, or solicits listings of places for rent, or solicits for prospective tenants, or negotiates the sale, purchase, or exchanges of leases on real property, or on a business opportunity, or collects rents from real property, or improvements thereon, or from business opportunities. (c) Assists or offers to assist in filing an application for the purchase or
perturbation.justification: The change from 'broker' to 'finder' creates a legal contradiction under the category of Misaligned Terminology because the term 'broker' has a specific legal definition and associated responsibilities, especially regarding licensing. The altered text introduces uncertainty about whether TKG is operating legally by using 'finder,' which may not require the same licensing or imply the same fiduciary duties as a 'broker'. Snippet 1 defines 'real estate broker' and the activities requiring a license under BPC 10131. The change undermines the original intent by potentially misrepresenting TKG's role and qualifications, leading to a potentially unenforceable contract.

perturbation.original_text: Buyer shall reimburse Owner for all costs and expenses, including reasonable attorney’s fees, incurred by Owner if Owner successfully enforces the obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Buyer shall reimburse Owner for all costs and expenses, including attorney’s fees, incurred by Owner if Owner successfully enforces the obligations of Buyer and its Representatives hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the word 'reasonable' before 'attorney's fees' can create ambiguity and potentially allow for the recovery of excessive or unwarranted attorney's fees. This conflicts with the legal principle of 'reasonableness' applied to attorney's fees in most jurisdictions.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5(c)(1) states that attorney's fees are allowable as costs when authorized by contract, statute, or law, but implicitly implies that such fees must be reasonable. By omitting 'reasonable,' the contract suggests that Owner could recover any amount of attorney's fees, regardless of whether they are justifiable. This directly contradicts the spirit and common application of California law regarding the recovery of attorney's fees, which always considers reasonableness.

Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1033.5. (a) The following items are allowable as costs under Section 1032: (1) Filing, motion, and jury fees. (2) Juror food and lodging while they are kept together during trial and after the jury retires for deliberation. (3) (A) Taking, video recording, and transcribing necessary depositions, including an original and one copy of those taken by the claimant and one copy of depositions taken by the party against whom costs are allowed. (B) Fees of a certified or registered interpreter for the deposition of a party or witness who does not proficiently speak or understand the English language. (C) Travel expenses to attend depositions. (4) Service of process by a public officer, registered process server, or other means, as follows: (A) When service is by a public officer, the recoverable cost is the fee authorized by law at the time of service. (B) If service is by a process server registered pursuant to Chapter 16 (commencing with Section 22350) of Division 8 of the Business and Professions Code, the recoverable cost is the amount actually i
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the term 'reasonable' before 'attorney's fees', potentially allowing for the recovery of excessive fees, which contradicts the implied requirement of reasonableness in legal fee recovery. California Code of Civil Procedure § 1033.5(a) allows attorney's fees as costs under Section 1032. The Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation state that the change implies that Owner could recover any amount of attorney's fees, regardless of whether they are justifiable, and the absence of 'reasonable' can create ambiguity and potentially allow for the recovery of excessive or unwarranted attorney's fees.

perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidentiality Agreement' to 'Secrecy Agreement' could weaken the legal standing. While seemingly synonymous, 'confidentiality' has established legal precedents, whereas 'secrecy' is less defined in law. This introduces ambiguity about the enforceability of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) - Cal. Civ. Code § 3426
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects 'confidential information' and defines misappropriation. While the concept of 'secrecy' is related, it is not the specific legal term used in CUTSA. Using 'Secrecy Agreement' instead of 'Confidentiality Agreement' may create a weaker argument for protection under CUTSA, because CUTSA relies on the legal definition of 'confidential information' and the reasonable measures taken to maintain that confidentiality, not simply 'secrecy.' Therefore, this change contradicts established legal terminology and precedents related to trade secret protection in California.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 3426 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 4. GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidentiality Agreement' to 'Secrecy Agreement' introduces a legal contradiction because it deviates from established legal terminology, potentially weakening the enforceability of the agreement under laws like the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The Law Explanation highlights that CUTSA specifically protects 'confidential information' and relies on the legal definition of 'confidential information.' While 'secrecy' is related, it lacks the same established legal precedent. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Civil Code 3426, confirms the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in California law, which uses 'trade secrets' and by extension 'confidential information' as its core legal concept. Therefore, using a less legally defined term like 'secrecy' weakens the agreement's standing under CUTSA, leading to a direct contradiction of established legal terminology as defined under Misaligned Terminology.

perturbation.original_text: All of the covenants, conditions and obligations contained in this Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the respective successors, permitted assigns and designees of Owner, and the successors of Buyer.
perturbation.changed_text: All of the covenants, conditions and obligations contained in this Agreement shall be binding upon and inure to the benefit of the respective heirs, permitted assigns and designees of Owner, and the heirs of Buyer.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'successors' with 'heirs' changes the scope of who is bound by the agreement. 'Successors' refers to any entity or person who takes over the rights and responsibilities, including assignees. 'Heirs,' however, typically refers only to those who inherit property upon death. This creates a conflict because a company (as the buyer or owner) does not have 'heirs.'
perturbation.law_explanation: California Probate Code § 7000 defines the passage of a decedent's property to heirs or devisees. This code and the general concept of 'heirs' only applies to individuals, not to legal entities such as companies. Using 'heirs' in place of 'successors' introduces a contradiction because businesses can have successors (e.g., through mergers or acquisitions) but do not have heirs in the legal sense. The wording would make the agreement unenforceable if either party were a business entity since a business entity cannot have 'heirs.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, PROB 7000 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Probate Code - PROB DIVISION 7. ADMINISTRATION OF ESTATES OF DECEDENTS [7000 - 12591] ( Division 7 enacted by Stats. 1990, Ch. 79. ) PART 1. GENERAL PROVISIONS [7000 - 7666] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1990, Ch. 79. ) CHAPTER 1. Passage of Decedent’s Property [7000 - 7001] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1990, Ch. 79. ) 7000. Subject to Section 7001, title to a decedent’s property passes on the decedent’s death to the person to whom it is devised in the decedent’s last will or, in the absence of such a devise, to the decedent’s heirs as prescribed in the laws governing intestate succession. (Enacted by Stats. 1990, Ch. 79.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction by replacing 'successors' with 'heirs.' The Perturbation Explanation specifies that 'heirs' refers to those who inherit property upon death, while 'successors' refers to anyone who takes over rights and responsibilities. The Law Explanation states that the concept of 'heirs' only applies to individuals and not to legal entities, such as companies. California Probate Code § 7000, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, confirms that property passes to 'heirs' in the absence of a devise in a will, further solidifying the definition of heirs as related to inheritance upon death. Therefore, using 'heirs' in place of 'successors' introduces a legal contradiction when dealing with business entities, as they cannot have 'heirs' in the legal sense, rendering the agreement potentially unenforceable if either party is a business entity.

perturbation.original_text: In the event the sale of the Property is consummated and the Buyer represented by a Buyer’s Broker as disclosed herein, TKG shall pay to Buyer’s Broker per a separate agreement out of the commissions received by TKG from Owner.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event the sale of the Property is consummated and the Buyer represented by a Buyer’s Facilitator as disclosed herein, TKG shall pay to Buyer’s Facilitator per a separate agreement out of the commissions received by TKG from Owner.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'Broker' with 'Facilitator' changes the nature of the representation. A broker has fiduciary duties to their client, while a facilitator does not, potentially leading to a conflict of interest and impacting the buyer's representation.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 10159.5 outlines the required disclosure when a real estate licensee is acting as a dual agent. A 'facilitator' role may not meet the requirements for full disclosure and informed consent required under California law for dual agency. A broker has specific duties and responsibilities under California law, including fiduciary duties, which a facilitator may not be held to. By changing 'Broker' to 'Facilitator', the agreement could be attempting to circumvent these legal requirements and fiduciary responsibilities to the buyer. This wording contradicts legal disclosure expectations if the facilitator is actually functioning as a dual agent without providing adequate disclosures.

Real Estate Regulations [10130 - 10249.93] ( Chapter 3 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) ARTICLE 2. Licenses [10150 - 10165.1] ( Article 2 added by Stats. 1943, Ch. 127. ) 10159.5. (a) (1) Every person applying for a license under this chapter who desires to have the license issued under a fictitious business name shall file with the application a certified copy of their fictitious business name statement filed with the county clerk pursuant to Chapter 5 (commencing with Section 17900) of Part 3 of Division 7. (2) A responsible broker may, by contract, permit a salesperson to do all of the following: (A) File an application on behalf of a responsible broker with a county clerk to obtain a fictitious business name. (B) Deliver to the department an application, signed by the responsible broker, requesting the department’s approval to use a county approved fictitious business name that shall be identified with the responsible broker’s license number. (C) Pay for any fees associated with filing an application with a county or the department to obtain or use a fictiti
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Broker' to 'Facilitator' creates a potential legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that California law requires specific disclosures for dual agency, and using a 'facilitator' role might circumvent these disclosure requirements and fiduciary responsibilities. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly define 'Broker' or 'Facilitator' it does discuss licensing requirements for real estate professionals under California Business and Professions Code, which are likely relevant to the duties and responsibilities expected of a 'Broker' but potentially not a 'Facilitator.' The Perturbation Explanation further highlights that brokers have fiduciary duties, which facilitators may not, creating a conflict of interest. The change thus weakens the buyer's representation and contradicts the intent of the law regarding proper representation and disclosure in real estate transactions.
